metadata.name: The Daily Reflection Tree
metadata.description: A deterministic self-reflection decision tree — situational questions, non-obvious signal mapping, true branching

node_types.question: Behavioral MCQ — 4 options, each emitting a signal. Questions are situational workplace moments, not abstract categories.
node_types.decision: Invisible routing — compares accumulated signal counts to branch the path
node_types.reflection: Mid-flow interpolated insight — references user's actual answer text
node_types.bridge: Axis transition with signal-aware variant text
node_types.summary: Final profile from 2³ = 8 archetypes, each with observation, tension, and closing question

a1_q1.text: You're walking back to your desk after a meeting that didn't go the way you expected. What's running through your head?

options.text: I'm replaying what I said — I think I lost them at the second point
The decision was already made before I walked in
I need to rethink my approach for the follow-up
No one in that room was actually listening

a1_q2.text: It's late afternoon and you realize you won't finish what you promised by end of day. What do you find yourself thinking?

options.text: I should have scoped this differently from the start
Three things that weren't mine to handle ate my morning
I'll rework the plan and deliver the most important piece tonight
The timeline was unrealistic — no one could have made this work

a1_q3.text: Your manager sends a brief, slightly critical message. You can't tell how serious it is. What happens in your body over the next ten minutes?

options.text: I start drafting what I'll say — facts, context, what I'd do differently
A knot forms. This will go badly no matter what I do
I set it aside until I have more information to respond to
I start wondering what else is going wrong that I haven't been told about

a1_q4_int.text: You've been reaching for the wheel all day. Think about the last thing that frustrated you — did you also feel a flicker of 'this is my fault, even though it probably isn't'?

options.text: Yes — I default to blame-myself-first. It's automatic
Not fault exactly, but I keep thinking about what I could have done
No — I was frustrated because someone else genuinely dropped the ball
I felt responsible for fixing it, even though I didn't cause it

a1_q4_ext.text: Think about one thing that went wrong today. Not the biggest thing — a small one. Is there a version of events where one different choice by you would have changed the outcome?

options.text: Honestly, maybe. I could have spoken up earlier
I've thought about it and no — the pieces weren't in my hands
There was a moment, but I didn't have enough information to act on it
Yes. I saw it coming and I didn't move

a1_q5.text: Someone gave you feedback today that felt off. Not cruel — just like they weren't seeing the full picture. What did you sit with afterward?

options.text: What part of it might be true, even if it stung
They don't know what I was dealing with when I made that choice
Whether I should have made my reasoning more visible earlier
The gap between how hard I tried and how it was received

a1_q6.text: There was a point today where the pressure was real. Not dramatic — just the low hum of too much to do. What did you actually do in that moment?

options.text: Picked the one thing I could finish and did it
Sat with it, feeling stuck, not sure where to start
Vented to someone — I needed to say it out loud
Reorganized my list and cut what wasn't essential

ref_a1_int.text: You said '{a1_q1}' after a meeting went sideways. And when pressure built, you '{a1_q6}'. There's a steady hand reaching for the wheel today. The question isn't whether you're driving — you are. The question is whether you let anyone else navigate.

ref_a1_ext.text: You said '{a1_q1}' after a meeting went sideways. And when pressure built, you '{a1_q6}'. Today has felt like weather — something happening around you, not through you. But notice: even describing it this way is a choice. That noticing is already agency.

a1_q7.text: Think of one moment today that went well. Not a big win — just a moment where things clicked. What made it work?

options.text: I made a specific choice that set it up
The right people happened to be in the room
I'd been thinking about it for a while and the preparation showed
Honestly, I got lucky — the timing worked out

a1_q8.text: A process or system made your work harder today. Something that shouldn't be this complicated, but is. What did you do with that friction?

options.text: Started thinking about how to change it, even a little
Pushed through it, but it wore me down
Found a workaround and moved on
Added it to the list of things that never get fixed around here

a1_q9.text: Someone pushed back on your work today. Not your effort — your actual output. The thing you made or decided. What happened inside you?

options.text: Curiosity first — what are they seeing that I'm not?
A flash of 'you don't understand what went into this'
I went quiet and started rethinking my approach
Frustration — the goalposts moved and no one told me

a1_q10_int.text: You keep reaching for the wheel. Here's the hard question: was there anything today that genuinely wasn't yours to carry — and you carried it anyway?

options.text: Yes. I took on something that wasn't mine because no one else would
I'm not sure where my responsibility ends and theirs begins anymore
Actually, yes — and I'm tired of it
Probably, but if I don't carry it, it falls

a1_q10_ext.text: You've described a day that pushed you around. But think small. Was there one moment — even sixty seconds — where you chose how to respond instead of just reacting?

options.text: There was. I paused before replying to something that annoyed me
I chose to let something go instead of fighting it
I can't think of one — I was in reaction mode all day
Maybe, but it felt so small it doesn't count

a1_q11.text: Think of one thing today that was genuinely out of your control. A rule, a decision, a reality you couldn't change. What did you do inside that constraint?

options.text: Found the one degree of freedom I still had and used it
Accepted it, but it cost me something to accept
Pushed back, even knowing it probably wouldn't change
Let it define the rest of my afternoon

a1_q12.text: Last question on this thread. Complete this sentence honestly: 'Today, I mostly…'

options.text: …chose how I showed up, even when the situation was hard
…responded to what came at me, as best I could
…shaped at least one thing that mattered to me
…got through it

a2_q1.text: A colleague you don't know well seemed off today — quieter than usual, maybe struggling with something. You noticed. What did you do with that noticing?

options.text: Went over and asked if they were okay
Thought about it for a second, then got back to my own work
Sent a brief message — nothing big, just an acknowledgment
Figured it wasn't my place. They'd ask if they needed something

a2_q2.text: You contributed meaningfully to something today, but when the story was retold, your name wasn't in it. What did that actually feel like?

options.text: Fine. The outcome mattered more than the attribution
A small sting, even though I know it shouldn't matter
I made a mental note. I didn't say anything, but I noticed
I made a point to mention someone else's contribution instead

a2_q3.text: Something tedious needed doing today — the kind of task that no one volunteers for. Be honest about what happened.

options.text: I did it before anyone had to ask
I waited to see if someone else would pick it up first
I did it, but I resented that it always seems to fall to me
I did it and genuinely didn't mind

a2_q4_con.text: You've been giving freely today. Here's the uncomfortable question: when you helped someone, was any part of it because you'd feel guilty if you didn't?

options.text: Probably yes. I don't like the feeling of not helping when I could
No — I wanted to. It wasn't obligation
A little. I worry about what people think if I don't step up
I don't analyze it — I just do what needs doing

a2_q4_ent.text: Think about today — not the big moments, the tiny ones. Did you hold a door, explain something twice, stay on a call an extra minute for someone else's sake?

options.text: Actually yes. I did something small I hadn't thought about until now
Not really — I was heads down on my own priorities
I think so, but it didn't feel like 'helping' — it was just normal
I was too stretched to give anything extra today

a2_q5.text: There was a moment today when you could have cut a corner and no one would have noticed. What happened?

options.text: I did it the right way anyway — it matters to me
I cut the corner. I needed the time more than the quality
I did it right, but I was aware that no one would notice or care
I didn't even think about cutting the corner

a2_q6.text: A team goal and a personal goal competed for your attention today. Only one could get your best energy. Which one got it?

options.text: The team goal — my personal stuff can wait
My personal goal — I need to protect my own trajectory
I tried to split it and neither got my best
The team goal, because their success pulls me forward too

ref_a2_con.text: You said '{a2_q1}' when someone seemed off. And when no one was watching, '{a2_q5}'. You've been investing in things that don't pay you back directly today. That's not common. The question worth sitting with: is this generosity, or is it a habit you can't stop?

ref_a2_ent.text: You said '{a2_q1}' when someone seemed off. And when no one was watching, '{a2_q5}'. You've been keeping close accounts today — tracking what's fair, what's yours, what's owed. That's not selfishness. It might be self-preservation. But notice the ledger.

a2_q7.text: Be honest about what you wanted from work today. Not what you should want — what you actually wanted when you sat down this morning.

options.text: To make something better than I found it
To get through my list and go home
To feel like my effort was seen and valued
To do something that mattered to someone other than me

a2_q8.text: Think of the most invisible thing you did today — something that kept the work moving but will never show up in a dashboard or a review. How do you feel about it?

options.text: Good. The work itself was the point
A little resentful, honestly. Invisible work doesn't get promoted
I didn't do anything invisible today — I focused on what's measurable
Satisfied, but I notice I wish someone knew

a2_q9_con.text: You give more than most people around you. You probably know this. What's the part of that you don't say out loud?

options.text: Sometimes I give because I don't know how to stop
I do keep a quiet tally, even though I don't act on it
I'm afraid that if I stop giving, I stop mattering
I've made peace with it. This is who I choose to be

a2_q9_ent.text: Forget the big gestures. Did you do one small thing today purely because it would make someone else's day slightly easier — with zero benefit to you?

options.text: Yes, and I'd forgotten about it until just now
I'm struggling to think of one
I did something, but it was partly self-serving too
Yes — and it was actually the best moment of my day

a2_q10.text: Someone you work closely with gets recognized for something you also shaped. In the thirty seconds after you hear the news — before you compose yourself — what's the first thing you feel?

options.text: Genuine happiness for them. They earned it
Happy for them, but a quiet voice asking 'what about me?'
I'm already thinking about what I need to do differently to be next
Proud — because I helped build what they were recognized for

a3_q1.text: You made a decision today that affected more than just you. In the moment you made it, whose face came to mind?

options.text: My own — how this would affect my workload, my standing
My team — specifically who would be helped or burdened by it
Someone downstream I've never met — a customer, a user, a patient
No one's face, honestly. I was thinking about the task, not the people

a3_q2.text: You and someone else saw a situation differently today. By the end of the conversation, could you state their position as well as they could?

options.text: Yes — I made a point to understand before I responded
Partially — I got the surface but probably missed what was underneath
No — I was focused on making my own point clear
I didn't try. I needed the outcome more than the understanding

a3_q3_oth.text: You've been holding other people in mind today. Is that a deliberate practice, or is it something else?

options.text: It's deliberate — I've trained myself to think past my own desk
It's mostly automatic — I just see how things connect to people
Honestly, it might be a way of avoiding my own stuff
Someone's situation today made it impossible not to think about them

a3_q3_self.text: Your focus has been close to home today. Try this: name one person who was affected by a choice you made today — someone you didn't think about at the time.

options.text: Actually, yes — someone comes to mind. I wasn't thinking of them
I'm sure someone was affected, but I genuinely don't know who
My immediate team, probably. I was too focused to check
I think my scope was the right scope today. Not everything needs to be wider

a3_q4.text: You ran into a problem today that you've seen before — same kind of issue, different day. What did you do with that pattern recognition?

options.text: Fixed the immediate instance and moved on
Mentioned it to someone who might be able to change the root cause
Started thinking about what would prevent this for everyone, not just me
Noticed the pattern but didn't have the energy to do anything with it

a3_q5.text: Something frustrated you today. In that frustrated moment, how much of the world could you see?

options.text: Just the thing in front of me. My field of vision narrowed
I was frustrated on behalf of my team, not just myself
I could see the systemic issue behind my frustration
Just me. I was the center of my own frustration

ref_a3_oth.text: You said '{a3_q2}' in a disagreement, and '{a3_q5}' when you were frustrated. Even under pressure, your awareness included other people. That's rare — and it's exhausting. Not everyone who sees widely has learned to rest within that seeing.

ref_a3_self.text: You said '{a3_q2}' in a disagreement, and '{a3_q5}' when you were frustrated. Today your lens stayed close — tight focus on what's right in front of you. That's not a failure of empathy. Sometimes the radius contracts because the center needs tending.

a3_q6.text: Think about the end product of your work — not your deliverable, but what it becomes when it reaches the person who actually uses it. How present was that person in your thinking today?

options.text: Very — I made at least one decision today with their experience in mind
They crossed my mind, but they didn't change what I did
Not at all. I was thinking about the people in the building, not outside it
I thought about them more than my immediate stakeholders, actually

a3_q7.text: Right now, in this moment — which of these feels most true about your work?

options.text: I have tasks. I complete them. That's the agreement
I'm part of something that extends well beyond my job description
My work creates ripples I can trace to real people
I do what's asked and I do it well. The bigger picture is someone else's job

a3_q8_oth.text: You've been holding a wide view today. Here's the question people with wide lenses tend to avoid: did you use that wider view to change something you did today, or did you just hold it and feel heavy?

options.text: I used it — it changed at least one decision I made
I held it. It felt important but I didn't act on it
Both — I acted on some of it and sat with the rest
Honestly, the wideness made it harder to act at all

a3_q8_self.text: Last question. Tomorrow, when you sit down to work — is there one person outside your immediate scope whose experience you could hold in mind? Not help. Not fix anything for. Just notice they exist.

options.text: Yes. Someone came to mind just now
I'd like to, but I know I'll forget as soon as things get busy
I think so. It's a small shift but it might change something
I think I need to take care of myself before I expand outward

variants.true_text:
You've been holding a wide lens — aware of people and systems beyond your immediate scope.

What follows isn't a score or a label. It's a reflection of the patterns you've described today.
variants.false_text:
You've been focused inward — processing your own experience with attention and honesty.

What follows isn't a judgment. It's a mirror made of the choices you've described.

start.text: A quiet space to observe how you showed up today.\n\nNo scores. No judgments. Just honest noticing.
start.heading: The Daily Reflection Tree

end.text: Reflection is a practice, not a destination.\n\nCome back tomorrow and see what's changed.
end.heading: Thank you for pausing.

internal_contribution_others.label: The Grounded Steward
internal_contribution_others.text: You took responsibility for your day, gave more than was asked, and held people in mind who weren't in the room. That's a rare combination — agency, generosity, and breadth in the same person on the same day.
internal_contribution_others.tension: Here's what stewards don't say out loud: the weight is real. You carry things that aren't yours because you can see them and no one else is picking them up. That's not sustainability. That's a slow leak.
internal_contribution_others.question: What would it look like to hold your standards — without holding everything?

internal_contribution_self.label: The Focused Builder
internal_contribution_self.text: You own your outcomes and you build with care. When things broke, you fixed them. When no one was watching, you did the work right anyway. Your focus was tight and productive today — energy pointed at the craft, not the crowd.
internal_contribution_self.tension: The edge of focused building is tunnel vision. You build well — but the person standing just outside your frame might have needed something from you today that you didn't see.
internal_contribution_self.question: Whose experience was quietly shaped by what you built today — and did you notice?

internal_entitlement_others.label: The Aware Contractor
internal_entitlement_others.text: You see the system clearly — who does what, who gets what, where the gaps are. You take ownership of your own work and you genuinely notice other people. But today, you were tracking the fairness of it all more than the giving.
internal_entitlement_others.tension: Awareness without generosity becomes surveillance. You see everything — but today you may have been keeping score more than keeping faith.
internal_entitlement_others.question: What would change if you gave one thing today without tracking whether it came back?

internal_entitlement_self.label: The Independent Driver
internal_entitlement_self.text: You trust yourself to deliver, and you did. You took ownership, set your own direction, and focused on what matters to you. The lens was on your work and your trajectory today.
internal_entitlement_self.tension: Self-reliance is a strength until it becomes a wall. Owning everything sounds like agency, but it can quietly close the door to receiving — help, perspective, or the kind of feedback that only lands when you're not armored.
internal_entitlement_self.question: Where might loosening your grip actually strengthen your position?

external_contribution_others.label: The Generous Adapter
external_contribution_others.text: You gave readily today — to your team, to the work, to people who needed something. You also felt the day pressing in, the circumstances shaping you more than you shaped them. Generous, but from a reactive posture.
external_contribution_others.tension: Generosity without agency can become self-erasure. You gave — but were you choosing to give, or just responding to whoever asked loudest?
external_contribution_others.question: What's one thing you gave today that you also chose — not just responded to?

external_contribution_self.label: The Dutiful Worker
external_contribution_self.text: You showed up and did your part. The conditions felt constraining, the scope felt predetermined, and your focus stayed on what was right in front of you. Your contribution was real, even if the conditions made it feel small.
external_contribution_self.tension: Duty without authorship is sustainable — but rarely fulfilling. The work gets done, the worker fades. Today felt more like keeping a promise to someone else's plan than building your own.
external_contribution_self.question: Where could you author one thing in your environment tomorrow — instead of just responding to it?

external_entitlement_others.label: The Frustrated Idealist
external_entitlement_others.text: You see what should be different — for the team, for the customer, for the system. The gap between what you observed and what arrived was real today. You care about fairness and you care about others. What you're short on is the lever.
external_entitlement_others.tension: Idealism without agency becomes resentment. You see the gap clearly — but watching it widens it. The frustration is evidence of caring. The question is what you do with it.
external_entitlement_others.question: What's the smallest action within reach that would close one gap you noticed today?

external_entitlement_self.label: The Depleted Observer
external_entitlement_self.text: Today happened to you more than through you. Energy was low, the environment felt immovable, and the lens narrowed to what was immediately in front of you. That's not a character flaw. That's what depletion looks like from the inside.
external_entitlement_self.tension: Depletion narrows everything — the lens, the agency, the generosity. When you're running on empty, even small choices become invisible. The danger isn't today. It's when today becomes every day.
external_entitlement_self.question: What's the smallest possible thing tomorrow that would feel like authorship — not productivity, just authorship?
